focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

Owen came back up from the undercroft to a kitchen stair filmed with soot. Dawn had got into the castle the way smoke did, through cracks that had not been there yesterday and under doors that no longer shut square. Somebody had slopped broth or potion or blood over the fourth step from the top and it had dried tacky in the grit. He kept one hand on the wall because the stone still moved fractionally under him, not in truth, not enough to throw him, but enough that his body had not yet accepted the battle was over above his head.

His right palm felt boiled inside the old burn. His nails had split at the white crescents. Blood had dried from his nose down the front of his jumper in a rusty fan, and each time he swallowed he could taste the chalk dust he had breathed below. The satchel strap bit into his shoulder. Inside it the Grinlow Leaves lay wrapped in a tea towel gone stiff with damp, and his ash-linen notebook pressed against his ribs like a second, smaller body.

The passage opened near the kitchens, but the smell from the Great Hall drew through the corridor and made a shape in the air before he reached it: smoke, old stone, singed wool, broth, blood, and the sweet wrong stink of spells burnt into masonry. He followed it because there was nowhere else to go that would count as coming back.

The Hall had stopped pretending to be a dining room.

The house tables had been shoved crooked against the walls to clear floor space. One trestle had lost a leg and leaned into Ravenclaw's bench at an angle that looked comic until Owen saw the dead laid in the gap beside it. Blankets, cloaks, table linen, a ripped curtain from somewhere above; anything that could cover a face had been pressed into use and none of it matched. Under the enchanted ceiling dawn sho...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...lm against the crack until blood slicked into the cut channel and the stone accepted it. The anchor answered with a hard descending hum that ran through the floor and up the walls. Somewhere above, conventional magic buckled in reply. He felt it in the undercroft as a long shiver of failed compatibility moving through stair, corridor, classroom, hall.

Good, he thought with a viciousness that surprised him. Answer, then.

He kept it in the only category he could bear: work. Chalk dried in the cuts, soot scraped his throat, and he forced line after line into the stone. Anything grander would have stopped him.

He built the next sentence smaller than the last, precise enough to survive his exhaustion. The repaired edge tightened. The hairline crack across the anchor's face drew down from a fingernail width to a thread-fine stubbornness, damage left visible even while the stone agreed to behave. The western side now held through impact after impact without opening all the way to brightness.

By the time he trusted that much, both sleeves were stiff with chalk and blood. The kitchen knife had fallen somewhere into the outer dark. The basin water was almost black. His hands no longer felt attached at the wrists in an entirely ordinary way. When he flexed his wand fingers, the air around them fuzzed faintly, as if the old work had left an afterimage in his magical signature and not yet decided whether to depart.

The hostile-wand branch stayed lit on the anchor's left face.

Once, as he shifted his weight, his sleeve brushed it and came away silver-grey with stone dust. He looked at the mark, then looked back to the seam.

Above him people kept killing and being killed by means he could have interrupted.

He pressed his bleeding palm back to the older wound and kept speaking.
